content.title: Derivative
content.content:
Let $f$ be defined on an interval containing $c$. The **derivative** 
 of $f$ at $c$ is
\[
f'(c) = \lim_{x \to c} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(c + h) - f(c)}{h}
\]
if this limit exists. We say $f$ is **differentiable** at $c$.

content.title: Differentiability Implies Continuity
content.content: If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f$ is continuous at $c$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content: $\lim_{x \to c} (f(x) - f(c)) = \lim_{x \to c} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \cdot (x - c) = f'(c) \cdot 0 = 0$.

The converse is false. The function $f(x) = |x|$ is continuous at $0$ but 
not differentiable there (the left and right derivatives differ).

content.title: Algebraic Differentiation Rules
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$, then:

1. $(f + g)'(c) = f'(c) + g'(c)$

2. $(kf)'(c) = kf'(c)$

3. **Product Rule:** $(fg)'(c) = f'(c)g(c) + f(c)g'(c)$

4. **Quotient Rule:** $(f/g)'(c) = \frac{f'(c)g(c) - f(c)g'(c)}{g(c)^2}$ (if $g(c) \neq 0$)
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$$
(fg)'(c) &= \lim_{x \to c} \frac{f(x)g(x) - f(c)g(c)}{x - c} \\
&= \lim_{x \to c} \frac{f(x)g(x) - f(x)g(c) + f(x)g(c) - f(c)g(c)}{x - c} \\
&= \lim_{x \to c} f(x) \cdot \frac{g(x) - g(c)}{x - c} + \lim_{x \to c} g(c) \cdot \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \\
&= f(c)g'(c) + g(c)f'(c). \qedhere
$$

content.title: Chain Rule
content.content:
If $g$ is differentiable at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $g(c)$, then 
$f \circ g$ is differentiable at $c$ and
\[
(f \circ g)'(c) = f'(g(c)) \cdot g'(c).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Define $\phi(y) = \frac{f(y) - f(g(c))}{y - g(c)}$ for $y \neq g(c)$ and 
$\phi(g(c)) = f'(g(c))$. Then $\phi$ is continuous at $g(c)$ and 
$f(y) - f(g(c)) = \phi(y)(y - g(c))$ for all $y$.

Substituting $y = g(x)$:
\[
\frac{f(g(x)) - f(g(c))}{x - c} = \phi(g(x)) \cdot \frac{g(x) - g(c)}{x - c}.
\]
Taking limits and using continuity of $\phi \circ g$:
\[
(f \circ g)'(c) = \phi(g(c)) \cdot g'(c) = f'(g(c)) \cdot g'(c). \qedhere
\]

content.title: Derivative of Inverse Functions
content.content:
Let $f$ be continuous and strictly monotone on an interval $I$, and 
differentiable at $c \in I$ with $f'(c) \neq 0$. Then $f^{-1}$ is 
differentiable at $f(c)$ and
\[
(f^{-1})'(f(c)) = \frac{1}{f'(c)}.
\]
content.label: Theorem
